edus.text: Obama ' s campaign hopes have always hinged on a steadily improving economy .
Job woes rattle Obama 's strategy
President Barack Obama had hoped to make the 2012 campaign a referendum on Mitt Romney ' s Bain record ,
Massachusetts stumbles
and what Obama ' s team sees as Romney ' s robotic oddness -
but the economy has other ideas .
Never mind battleground polls , super PAC spending or the number of campaign offices
opened in the greater Columbus , Ohio , metropolitan area .
The only metrics
that matter to Obama
- jobs
created and lost -
are suddenly headed in a very dangerous direction .
Obama ' s campaign hopes have always hinged on a steadily improving economy and a growing sense
that things are moving in the right direction after the Bush hangover .
Earlier this year , the economy seemed to be cooperating .
But May ' s 69,000 job-creation number , an unemployment uptick to 8.2 percent and downward revisions on earlier , rosier reports have upended that rationale .
Renewed concerns about the global recovery ,
compounded by the slow-motion fiscal train wreck in Europe , a slowdown in China and Friday ' s Dow selloff
that put the index in negative territory for the year
have seriously undercut Obama ' s main reelection strategy :
Making the race a referendum on Romney .
Obama said Friday in Golden Valley , Minn. ,
that
while the economy is " not growing as fast
as we want it to grow ...
we will come back stronger ;
we do have better days ahead . "
But time is running out for a " V " shaped upturn
that would power Obama to a second term .
" The president has to address what he is going to do
to change things ,
rather than staying the same policy course
he charted
which has brought us to this point , "
GOP pollster David Winston said .
" This was a bad report , not only in terms of the limited jobs
created and the increase in the unemployment rate
but the revision downward of the previous two months as well .
This comes on top of the economic growth rate being revised downward to 1.9 percent . "
The lousy report seemed like a flashback to 2011 ,
when an encouraging spring led to a disappointing summer jobs picture , especially among young people
- a core Obama constituency .
Teenagers ,
according to the May report ,
now have an unemployment rate
approaching 25 percent .
Republicans
- unbowed by Obama ' s charge
that they are rooting for failure -
greeted the numbers with a barrage of emails , press releases and media availabilities .
House Majority Leader Eric Cantor
( R-Va. )
called the report " pathetic , "
while a hoarse Speaker John Boehner
( R-Ohio )
shouted ,
" Why don ' t they pick up the bills
and pass them ? " - a reference to the jobs bills
introduced by Republicans in lieu of Obama ' s own stymied jobs package .
And Romney
- whose Boston campaign office is festooned with mock street signs
touting the overly optimistic job creation claim of the 2009 stimulus -
released a strongly worded statement
minutes after the numbers posted on a federal Web site .
" Today ' s weak jobs report is devastating news for American workers and American families , "
Romney said .
" This week has seen a cascade of one bad piece of economic news after another .

Slowing GDP growth , plunging consumer confidence , an increase in unemployment claims , and now another dismal jobs report all stand as a harsh indictment of the president ' s handling of the economy . ...
The president ' s re-election slogan may be ' forward , '
but it seems like we ' ve been moving backward .
" We can do so much better in America .
That ' s why I ' m running for president . "
